title: Superconductivity-Enabled Conversion of Ferromagnetic Resonance into Standing Spin Waves
authors: Ya. V. Turkin, N. G. Pugach, F. M. Maksimov, A. S. Pakhomov, A. I. Chernov, V. I. Belotelov, S. N. Polulyakh, V. S. Stolyarov
abstract: Superconductors can transport spin without Joule dissipation, yet their coherent coupling to short-wavelength magnons in insulating magnets remains largely unexplored. Here we demonstrate experimentally and theoretically that a conventional diffusive superconductor can enable the conversion of the uniform ferromagnetic-resonance (FMR) mode into perpendicular standing spin waves (PSSWs) in an adjacent ferrimagnetic insulator. In Bi-substituted iron-garnet/Nb bilayers, the microwave transmission develops an additional resonance feature that appears only below the Nb transition temperature and lies close to the uniform FMR peak. A microscopic theory that self-consistently couples the quasiclassical Keldysh--Usadel description of the superconducting condensate to the Landau--Lifshitz--Gilbert dynamics shows that the conversion requires two ingredients: (i) an interfacial spin-transfer torque mediated by spin-polarized triplet Cooper pairs and (ii) a depth-dependent effective field produced by Abrikosov vortices (electromagnetic proximity). The resulting susceptibility reproduces the measured lineshapes and establishes superconductivity as an active control knob for exchange standing-wave modes in magnetic insulators.
summary: This paper demonstrates that a superconductor can act as a control knob to excite high-frequency spin waves in an adjacent insulator. By utilizing both triplet-mediated spin torque and vortex-induced field gradients, the researchers show how to convert uniform magnetic resonance into complex standing wave modes.
main_problem: The study explores how a conventional superconductor can be used to actively control and convert uniform ferromagnetic resonance (FMR) into short-wavelength perpendicular standing spin waves (PSSWs) in an adjacent magnetic insulator.
main_result: The researchers demonstrate that superconductivity enables mode conversion through a dual mechanism: interfacial spin-transfer torque from triplet Cooper pairs and symmetry breaking via vortex-induced electromagnetic proximity.
method: The work combines broadband microwave spectroscopy on Bi-GdIG/Nb bilayers with a microscopic theory coupling the Keldysh-Usadel formalism for the superconductor to the Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert dynamics for the magnet.
model_or_system: The physical system is a bilayer of Bi-substituted iron-garnet (Bi-GdIG) and Niobium (Nb) in the Abrikosov vortex state. The theoretical model uses a linearized 1D LLG equation with a Robin-type boundary condition at the interface and a depth-dependent effective field from vortices.
key_observables: Complex microwave transmission (S21 amplitude) and transverse magnetic susceptibility.
important_parameters: Temperature (4K to 11K), external magnetic field, frequency (4 GHz), exchange stiffness, and spin-mixing conductance.
main_assumptions: The model assumes a diffusive Nb film, a linearized response regime, and attributes peak asymmetry to an unmodeled magnetostatic field from the Gadolinium substrate.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the sample geometry and setup; Figure 2 compares microwave transmission in uncapped vs. bilayer films, highlighting the new resonance peak below Tc; Figure 3 shows the calculated susceptibility and mode hybridization; Supplementary figures show temperature-dependent transmission and FMR peak shifts.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of magnon control, describes the experimental bilayer setup, presents the observed resonance emergence below Tc, develops a microscopic theory (Keldysh-Usadel + LLG) to explain the dual mechanism of torque and vortex-induced asymmetry, and concludes with a discussion on tunability.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Sample and measurement geometry. A Bi- substituted iron-garnet film is capped by a Nb layer and placed on a coplanar waveguide. The microwave magnetic field hAC drives magnetization precession about the out-of- plane static field Hext.

FIG. 2. Normalized microwave transmission |S21| versus out-of-plane field at f = 4 GHz for representative temperatures T = 11 K (normal state), T = 8 K (near Tc), and T = 4 K (deep in the superconducting state). Left column: uncapped Bi-GdIG film (experiment). Right column: Bi-GdIG/Nb bilayer (experiment, blue) together with the calculated lineshape (theory, orange). Below Tc, the bilayer develops an additional resonance feature (the “right” peak) near the uniform FMR position, while the uncapped film shows only the conventional response.For more details see Supplemental Material [30]

FIG. 3. Calculated transverse susceptibility χxy of the Bi-GdIG/Nb structure versus frequency and field. The dashed line indicates the uniform-mode (Kittel) dispersion. Proximity-enabled hybridization with the lowest PSSW pro- duces two nearby resonant branches, consistent with the two- peak structure observed in Fig. 2.

Spintronics.note: The paper is highly relevant as it directly studies the interface of magnetism and superconductivity, specifically focusing on how superconducting properties (vortices and triplet pairs) control magnon/spin-wave modes.
